title: /key
text: /key Build keyboard translation tables in a character-based interface. /key FileName Description Note: The /key functionality is intended only for character-based interfaces. It cannot be used with Windows because Windows handles keyboard codes in a radically different manner. For more information, see X11 Keyboards . The escape sequences sent by a keyboard are often rather cryptic and difficult to decipher. Instead of entering each sequence, the /key switch creates keyboard translation table definitions by asking a series of questions, which builds a keyboard translation table from keyboard input to FileName . This facility is designed to provide a good start for defining keyboard translation tables; it is not intended for defining whole tables. For example, each macro can only be defined once and special font characters are not included. To create mappings, you press the key or series of keys for each function, Use the /key switch to create a keyboard translation table, then use the following command line switches to compile it: /tra /fil FileName Using /key Run the Uniface IDE using /key ide /key mykeys Copy The /key facility starts by giving its general syntax rules. Define up to ten macros which can be used in further definitions. In response to the prompts, enter a keystroke or series of keystrokes for each Uniface function. Each function can have any number of keystroke combinations. A keyboard translation table definition is created and saves in the specified file. After creating the file, do one of the following: If you have Uniface running on a system for which you already have a keyboard translation, create a new table and use the ^INS_FILE structure editor function to read in the file. (The SUPER key combination for ^INS_FILE is GOLD Space I X.) If you do not have another keyboard translation available, compile the table using the /tra command line switch. For example: ide /tra /fil mykeys Copy This command creates a table called TERMFILE. Declare TERMFILE as your keyboard in the assignment file which you use to start the Uniface IDE. For example: $KEYBOARD = TERMFILE Copy You can give the new keyboard translation table TERMFILE another name. Related Links Keyboard Translation Table Syntax Keyboard translation tables map input codes from keyboards or database to logical Uniface functions and characters. Macro Definitions The translation table compiler supports up to ten macros, ^M0 through ^M9, for commonly-used combinations. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
